stem: Which river divides Bordeaux into its Left Bank and Right Bank wine regions?

options.text: The Dordogne
The Garonne
The Gironde
The Loire

explanation: The **Gironde estuary** divides Bordeaux into its Left Bank (Médoc, Graves) and Right Bank (Saint-Émilion, Pomerol). The Gironde is formed by the confluence of the Garonne and Dordogne rivers upstream.

stem: Which grape variety dominates red wine blends on Bordeaux's Left Bank?

options.text: Merlot
Cabernet Franc
Cabernet Sauvignon
Malbec

explanation: **Cabernet Sauvignon** dominates Left Bank blends, particularly in the Médoc appellations (Pauillac, Saint-Estèphe, Saint-Julien, Margaux). The cooler, gravelly soils suit its late ripening. Merlot dominates the Right Bank instead.

stem: The 1855 Classification ranked châteaux primarily on the basis of what criterion?

options.text: Soil composition and terroir quality
Price achieved at market
Wine critic scores
Vineyard size and yield

explanation: The **1855 Classification** ranked 61 Médoc châteaux (plus Haut-Brion from Graves) into five growths based primarily on the **price their wines commanded at the time** — considered a reliable proxy for quality. It remains largely unchanged today, with Mouton Rothschild's promotion to First Growth in 1973 the sole exception.

stem: What is the primary red grape variety of Burgundy?

options.text: Cabernet Sauvignon
Gamay
Pinot Noir
Syrah

explanation: **Pinot Noir** is the sole permitted red grape in the Côte d'Or appellations of Burgundy. Gamay is used in Beaujolais (technically part of greater Burgundy) and in Bourgogne Passetoutgrains blends.

stem: Which appellation accounts for the largest volume of Chablis production?

options.text: Petit Chablis
Chablis
Chablis Premier Cru
Chablis Grand Cru

explanation: The straight **Chablis** appellation accounts for the largest share of production. Petit Chablis covers less favourable outlying soils, while Premier Cru and Grand Cru represent progressively smaller, higher-quality sites. Grand Cru covers only around 100 hectares across seven named climats.

stem: Which soil type is most associated with the grands crus of the Côte de Nuits?

options.text: Sandy loam over granite
Clay and limestone over Jurassic bedrock
Volcanic basalt
Alluvial gravel over clay

explanation: The grands crus of the Côte de Nuits sit on **clay and limestone soils over Jurassic-era limestone bedrock**. The mid-slope position provides ideal drainage. The precise mix of clay and limestone at each site is a key determinant of style — more clay gives fuller, richer wines; more limestone gives finesse.

stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three main grape varieties permitted in Champagne?

options.text: Chardonnay
Pinot Gris
Pinot Noir
Pinot Meunier

explanation: The three main Champagne varieties are **Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier**. Pinot Gris is not among them, though it is one of several minor varieties also technically permitted. A Blanc de Blancs is made solely from Chardonnay; a Blanc de Noirs from the red-skinned varieties only.

stem: What does the term 'Non-Vintage' (NV) mean in the context of Champagne?

options.text: The wine is made entirely from a single harvest year
The wine is blended across multiple harvest years using reserve wines
The wine is produced from a single Grand Cru village
The wine has not been aged on its lees

explanation: **Non-Vintage Champagne** is blended across multiple years using reserve wines from previous harvests. This allows houses to maintain a consistent house style regardless of annual vintage variation. NV must be aged for a minimum of 15 months, of which at least 12 must be on the lees.

stem: Which grape variety is responsible for the red wines of Hermitage and Côte-Rôtie in the Northern Rhône?

options.text: Grenache
Mourvèdre
Syrah
Cinsault

explanation: **Syrah** is the sole red grape of the Northern Rhône, producing the celebrated reds of Hermitage, Côte-Rôtie, Crozes-Hermitage, and Saint-Joseph. In Côte-Rôtie, small amounts of white Viognier may be co-fermented with Syrah to add aromatic complexity.

stem: What is the dominant grape variety in Châteauneuf-du-Pape red wines?

options.text: Syrah
Mourvèdre
Grenache
Cinsault

explanation: **Grenache** is the dominant variety in Châteauneuf-du-Pape, typically forming the backbone of blends. Up to 18 grape varieties are permitted in the appellation. Syrah and Mourvèdre are common blending partners, adding structure and colour.

stem: At what stage is grape spirit added during the production of Port wine?

options.text: Before fermentation begins
During fermentation, to arrest it
After fermentation is complete
During barrel ageing

explanation: Grape spirit (aguardente) is added **during fermentation**, typically when the wine reaches around 6–8% alcohol. This kills the yeast and arrests fermentation, leaving residual sugar in the wine. The resulting Port is sweet, with the spirit raising the final alcohol to around 19–22% ABV.

stem: Which style of Sherry is completely dry and produced under a layer of flor yeast?

options.text: Oloroso
Amontillado
Pedro Ximénez
Fino

explanation: **Fino** Sherry is completely dry and aged entirely under a protective layer of flor — a film of yeast that forms on the wine's surface. The flor consumes glycerol and protects the wine from oxidation, resulting in a pale, delicate, and bone-dry style. Manzanilla is a Fino produced specifically in Sanlúcar de Barrameda.

stem: What are the two sections that make up the WSET Level 3 Systematic Approach to Tasting Wine?

options.text: Appearance and Nose
Nose and Palate
Description and Conclusions
Palate and Conclusions

explanation: The SAT is divided into a **descriptive** section (covering Appearance, Nose, and Palate) and an evaluative **Conclusions** section (covering Quality Level and Readiness for Drinking). Options a, b, and d each name only sub-sections within the descriptive part rather than the two top-level divisions.

stem: How many points does the Clarity scale in the SAT Appearance section contain?

options.text: Two: clear and hazy
Three: clear, medium, and hazy
Five: matching the standard medium scale
Three: clear, cloudy, and opaque

explanation: **Clarity** uses only a two-point scale — clear or hazy — making it one of the exceptions in the SAT. Most other structural components use a five-point scale subdivided around medium. Haziness may indicate a fault, but deliberately hazy wines will not appear in the Level 3 examination.

stem: Which colour term describes a red wine that shows a noticeable blue or purple hue?

options.text: Garnet
Tawny
Ruby
Purple

explanation: The SAT colour scale for red wines runs from **purple** through ruby, garnet, tawny, to brown. Purple is selected when a noticeable blue or purple colour is present. Ruby is the most common red wine colour and has no blue cast; garnet indicates an orange or brown tint.

stem: In the SAT Wine-Lexicon, which secondary aroma cluster includes vanilla, toast, cedar, and charred wood?

options.text: Yeast (lees, autolysis)
MLF
Deliberate oxidation
Oak

explanation: The **Oak** cluster in the secondary aromas section of the Wine-Lexicon covers vanilla, cloves, nutmeg, coconut, toast, cedar, charred wood, smoke, chocolate, coffee, and resinous notes. MLF covers buttery and creamy characters; yeast/autolysis covers biscuit and brioche; deliberate oxidation is a tertiary cluster.

stem: Why does the SAT Level 3 subdivide 'medium' into medium(-), medium, and medium(+) for most scales?

options.text: To align the SAT with international competition judging standards
Because most wine observations fall within the medium range, so greater precision is useful there
To give tasters a way to avoid committing to high or low scores
Because the Level 2 three-point scale was found to produce inaccurate results at the extremes

explanation: The SAT explains that **the majority of observations for the majority of wines fall within the medium range**, making it valuable to subdivide it for precision. The scale is not five equal parts — medium(+) means 'medium, but towards the upper end of the medium band'. Tasters are still encouraged to use the full extremes confidently when warranted.

stem: A white wine displays prominent aromas of petrol, honey, and mushroom. How should its aroma development be classified?

options.text: Youthful
Developing
Fully developed
Tired/past its best

explanation: Petrol, honey, and mushroom are all **tertiary aromas** arising from bottle ageing. When tertiary aromas predominate, the SAT classifies the wine as fully developed, even if some primary or secondary aromas remain. 'Developing' applies when some tertiary aromas are present but primary and secondary aromas still dominate.

stem: Why does the SAT recommend assessing the colour of white wines at the core of the glass rather than at the rim?

options.text: The core reveals oxidation more reliably than the rim does
White wines appear nearly colourless at the rim, so colour is more easily judged where the liquid is deeper
Rim colour in white wines indicates the presence of residual sugar
Core colour is used to estimate alcohol level in white wines

explanation: White and most rosé wines appear **very pale, almost colourless at the rim** because the liquid depth is shallowest there. Colour is therefore assessed at the core, where there is sufficient depth for it to be judged clearly. By contrast, red wines are assessed near the rim because their pigmentation can make the core appear opaque.

stem: According to the SAT quality framework, a wine rated 'very good' shows positively against how many of the four quality criteria?

options.text: All four criteria
Two criteria, falling short on two
Three criteria, falling short on one
One criterion, falling short on three

explanation: The SAT maps each quality level onto the four criteria of balance, intensity, length, and complexity. **Outstanding** scores positively on all four; **very good** on three; **good** on two; **acceptable** on one; and **poor** shows problems across all four criteria. Option a describes outstanding; option b describes good.

stem: A taster assesses a sweet wine as having medium(-) acidity. According to SAT guidance, what is the most likely explanation for this being a misleading assessment?

options.text: Sweet wines always have low acidity by definition
High sweetness can mask the perception of acidity, making objectively high acidity appear lower than it is
The five-point acidity scale does not apply to sweet wines
Alcohol in sweet wines suppresses the tingling sensation associated with acidity

explanation: The SAT explicitly warns that **high sweetness and high acidity can mask each other**. In a sweet wine, acidity serves to balance the sugar rather than dominating as a single feature, so it appears less obvious than in a dry high-acid wine such as Chablis. The mouth-watering effect remains a reliable guide to the actual acidity level regardless of sweetness, and tasters should try to assess the actual level rather than the apparent one.

stem: How does the SAT distinguish 'too old' from 'drink now: not suitable for ageing or further ageing'?

options.text: Too old applies only to wines over 20 years; drink now covers wines between 10 and 20 years
Too old applies to wines never suitable for ageing; drink now applies to wines that were once age-worthy
In a wine described as too old, negative changes dominate; drink now means the wine is near the end of its drinkable life but negative changes do not yet dominate
The two terms are interchangeable in the SAT and either may be selected

explanation: The SAT draws a precise line: **'drink now: not suitable for ageing or further ageing'** covers wines that have undergone ageing but are at or near the end of their drinkable life — further change is unlikely to be positive, but the wine remains acceptable. **'Too old'** is reserved for wines where negative changes have come to dominate entirely. Option b describes a different distinction not made in the SAT materials.

stem: Which term describes the colour of a white wine that shows a noticeable greenish tint?

options.text: Lemon
Gold
Lemon-green
Amber

explanation: The SAT white wine colour scale runs from **lemon-green** through lemon, gold, amber, to brown. Lemon-green is selected when there is a noticeable greenish quality to the colour. Lemon is the most common white wine colour and has no green tint; gold indicates a hint of orange or brown.

stem: What wine fault gives aromas often described as vinegar or nail polish remover?

options.text: TCA (cork taint)
Reduction
Volatile acidity
Oxidation

explanation: **Volatile acidity (VA)** at high levels produces aromas commonly described as vinegar or nail polish remover. All wines contain some VA, and low levels can add fragrance and complexity. TCA produces damp cardboard aromas; reduction gives stinky, sulphurous characters; oxidation produces toffee, honey, and caramel notes.

stem: What volume of wine does the SAT recommend pouring as a tasting sample?

options.text: 2 cL
5 cL
10 cL
15 cL

explanation: The SAT recommends a **5 cL sample**. This is sufficient to assess appearance, nose, and palate, but small enough that the wine can be swirled in the base of the bowl without spilling. Consistent pour volumes across glasses are also emphasised.

stem: Which sweetness term is used for a wine where sugar has become the wine's most prominent feature, covering most classic sweet wines such as Sauternes and Port?

options.text: Medium-sweet
Off-dry
Sweet
Luscious

explanation: **Sweet** is the SAT term for wines where the presence of sugar has become the prominent feature, encompassing classic dessert wines including Sauternes and Port. Luscious is reserved for wines with even higher sugar levels that are notably more viscous and leave a sticky sensation; medium-sweet covers wines with distinct sugar that still falls short of most desserts.

stem: According to the SAT, where on the palate is acidity most strongly detected?

options.text: On the tip of the tongue
At the back of the throat
On the sides of the tongue
On the gums above the front teeth

explanation: The SAT states that for most people, **acidity is detected most strongly at the sides of the tongue**, where it causes a sharp, tingling sensation and makes the mouth water. The gums above the front teeth are the recommended area for detecting tannins, not acidity.

stem: Which wine fault gives aromas reminiscent of damp cardboard and causes fruit flavours to appear muted?

options.text: Reduction
TCA (cork taint)
Oxidation
Brettanomyces

explanation: **TCA (Trichloroanisole)** is the fault associated with damp cardboard aromas and muted fruit. One of its main causes is a tainted cork, which is why wines affected by TCA are often described as 'corked'. Reduction gives stinky, sulphurous characters; oxidation gives toffee and honey notes; Brettanomyces gives plastic or animal aromas.

stem: Which mousse descriptor applies to a sparkling wine whose bubbles are very soft and fine, often because it has undergone extensive ageing?

options.text: Aggressive
Creamy
Delicate
Petillant

explanation: The SAT mousse scale is delicate – creamy – aggressive. **Delicate** describes sparkling wines with very soft, fine bubbles, typically found in wines that have undergone extensive ageing or have been bottled at lower than typical dissolved CO₂ pressure. Aggressive describes young wines that seem to explode on the palate; creamy is the expected norm for most sparkling wines.

stem: What alcohol level, as a percentage abv, is classified as 'high' for a standard (non-fortified) wine in the SAT?

options.text: Above 12% abv
Above 13% abv
14% abv and above
15% abv and above

explanation: The SAT defines **high alcohol** for standard wines as 14% abv and above. Medium covers 11–13.9% abv and low covers below 11% abv. Note that fortified wines use a separate three-point scale starting at 15% abv.

stem: A taster notices that a wine's aromas were dominated by fresh red fruit on the nose, but earthy and spicy notes are more prominent on the palate. According to the SAT, why does this occur?

options.text: Palate perception always overrides nose perception for any given wine
The warming of wine in the mouth makes earthy, spicy, and toasty characteristics more prominent, while fruity and floral notes can become less so
It indicates the wine is developing and tertiary aromas are replacing primary ones
Swirling releases different aromatic compounds than warming does, causing the discrepancy

explanation: The SAT explains that **warming of the wine in the mouth** can make some characteristics more apparent than on the nose. Specifically, earthy, spicy, and toasty characteristics tend to become more prominent on the palate, while fruity and floral characteristics can be less prominent. This is a normal observation and does not indicate a fault or a change in development stage.

stem: Which of the following best explains why legs (tears) in a wine glass are more persistent and thicker in some wines than others?

options.text: Wines with higher acidity produce more persistent legs due to increased surface tension
Wines containing sugar or high alcohol are more viscous and therefore produce thicker, more persistent legs
Deeper-coloured wines always produce more prominent legs than pale wines
Legs are produced by dissolved carbon dioxide escaping from the wine

explanation: The SAT states that **wines containing sugar or high alcohol levels are more viscous**, which results in thicker and more persistent legs. Legs are streams of liquid that adhere to the glass after swirling and are sometimes also called tears. Colour depth and acidity do not determine leg thickness.

stem: According to the SAT, which component of wine does NOT change (or changes very little) as the wine ages in bottle?

options.text: Fruit aromas
Tannin levels
Alcohol level
The perception of sweetness

explanation: The SAT explicitly states that **the alcohol level does not change** as a wine ages in bottle. Acidity and sugar levels change very little, though sweet wines very slowly taste drier over time. Tannins soften with ageing, and primary fruit aromas develop towards tertiary characteristics.

stem: What are the four criteria used in the SAT to assess a wine's quality level?

options.text: Colour, aroma intensity, finish, and tannin
Balance, intensity, length, and complexity
Acidity, tannin, alcohol, and body
Development, balance, finish, and sweetness

explanation: The SAT uses four criteria to determine quality level: **balance**, **intensity** (flavour concentration), **length** (finish), and **complexity**. The quality rating from acceptable to outstanding is determined by how many of these criteria a wine scores positively on. Option c describes structural components assessed on the palate, not quality criteria.

stem: Brettanomyces ('Brett') is a yeast that can produce which type of aromas in wine?

options.text: Damp cardboard and muted fruit
Toffee, honey, and caramel
Plastic or animal notes such as sticking plasters, leather, or sweaty horses
Rotten eggs and boiled cabbage

explanation: **Brettanomyces** produces plastic or animal aromas described in the SAT as sticking plasters, hot vinyl, smoked meat, leather, or sweaty horses. Some consumers find low levels acceptable or even desirable. Rotten eggs and boiled cabbage are associated with reduction; damp cardboard with TCA; toffee and honey with oxidation.

stem: Why should a taster be well hydrated before conducting a SAT tasting session?

options.text: Dehydration lowers the perception of tannin, making wines seem softer than they are
Dehydration can cause nasal aroma receptors to dry out and lose sensitivity to aromas
Dehydration raises the apparent alcohol level of all wines tasted
Water neutralises residual flavours between wines more effectively than bread

explanation: The SAT states that **dehydration may cause nasal aroma receptors to become dry and lose their sensitivity to aromas**. It also notes that dehydration is easy to develop during a tasting session because saliva is lost every time wine is spat out. Dehydration also reduces the mouth-watering response used to judge acidity, though this is a secondary concern noted separately in the palate section.

stem: In the SAT, what does 'body' represent when assessing a wine on the palate?

options.text: The weight of tannins alone, assessed on the gums
The overall textural impression created by all structural components working together
The alcohol level expressed as a percentage
The combination of colour intensity and flavour concentration

explanation: The SAT defines **body** (or mouthfeel) as the overall textural impression created by all structural components working together — not any single component. Alcohol is typically the main contributor, but sugar, acidity, and tannin all play a role. High acidity makes a wine feel lighter in body; high alcohol and ripe tannins push it towards full-bodied.

stem: Which tertiary aroma cluster in the Wine-Lexicon includes petrol, kerosene, honey, and mushroom descriptors?

options.text: Fruit development (red)
Deliberate oxidation
Bottle age (white)
Bottle age (red)

explanation: The **Bottle age (white)** cluster in the tertiary aromas section of the Wine-Lexicon covers petrol, kerosene, cinnamon, ginger, nutmeg, toast, nutty, mushroom, hay, and honey. Bottle age (red) covers leather, forest floor, tobacco, and farmyard characters. Deliberate oxidation covers almond, marzipan, and toffee.

stem: The SAT states that 'high' is a range, not an absolute extreme. What practical implication does this have for a candidate assessing a wine with notably high acidity?

options.text: The candidate should only use 'high' if the wine is the most acidic they have ever tasted
The candidate should default to medium(+) unless the wine is noticeably atypical even within high-acid styles
The candidate should be confident using 'high' when the acidity is a remarkable feature, without waiting for an extreme example
The candidate should use 'high' only for wines from cool climates, never for warm-climate wines

explanation: The SAT explicitly encourages candidates to **use the ends of the scale confidently**, reminding them that 'high' is itself a range and should not be reserved solely for wines at the absolute extreme. The materials warn against over-using medium through reluctance to commit, noting that tasting notes dominated by mediums fail to capture the true character of wines. Option b describes exactly the over-cautious behaviour the SAT cautions against.

stem: A candidate writes 'ruby-garnet' to describe a red wine's colour in the SAT examination. How will this be marked?

options.text: The candidate will receive marks for both ruby and garnet if the examiner considers both valid
The candidate will not receive the mark because a range is not specific enough under SAT rules
The candidate will receive marks only if ruby is listed first
The candidate will be penalised one mark for using a hyphenated range

explanation: The SAT is explicit: for hyphenated scale lines, the candidate **must select one and only one term**. Using a range such as 'ruby-garnet' or 'ruby to garnet' means the examiner will not award the mark because the answer is not specific enough. Notably, if both ruby and garnet are considered valid descriptions the examiner would award marks for either one — but only if the candidate commits to a single choice.

stem: According to the SAT, how does assessing tannin quantity differ from assessing tannin quality, and why does this matter when calibrating scores?

options.text: Tannin quantity is only relevant for red wines; tannin quality is assessed for both reds and whites

Unripe tannins feel aggressively astringent even at medium levels, while ripe tannins at high levels may feel velvety and show little astringency — the level must be judged independently of the texture
Tannin quality is determined by the grape variety, while tannin quantity is determined by maceration time
High tannin quantity always means the wine feels rougher and thinner on the palate

explanation: The SAT warns that **unripe tannins are more aggressively astringent even at medium levels, whereas ripe tannins at high levels can feel velvety and show little astringency**. This means astringency alone is not a reliable proxy for tannin quantity. The material gives the example of a basic Pinot Noir from barely-ripe grapes (medium tannin, harsh texture) versus a high-quality Shiraz from a hot region (high tannin, velvety texture). Candidates must assess the actual level, not the perceived harshness.

stem: The SAT notes that simplicity is not always a negative in a wine and that complexity can sometimes detract from quality. Which type of wine is cited as an example where purity of expression may outweigh complexity?

options.text: Vintage Port
Red Rioja Reserva
Icewines
Aged Tawny Port

explanation: The SAT explicitly names **icewines** as an example where purity and clarity of expression can make a wine great, and where the addition of oak or tertiary characters might actually detract from quality rather than add to it. This illustrates that the four quality criteria are not always all weighted equally, and that simplicity with intensity and purity can justify a high quality rating.

stem: Which two components in food tend to make wines taste 'harder' — more astringent, more acidic, and less fruity?

options.text: Salt and acid
Bitterness and chilli heat
Sweetness and umami
Fat and umami

explanation: The materials identify **sweetness and umami** as the two food components that make wines taste harder — increasing perceived bitterness, astringency, and acidity while decreasing body, sweetness, and fruitiness. Salt and acid have the opposite effect, making wines taste softer.

stem: What effect does salt in food have on the perception of a wine?

options.text: It increases the perception of body and decreases astringency and bitterness
It increases the perception of astringency and bitterness
It decreases the perception of body and fruitiness
It has no significant effect on how wine is perceived

explanation: According to the materials, **salt in food increases the perception of body in the wine and decreases the perception of astringency, bitterness, and acidity**. It is a wine-friendly component that can enhance fruit character and soften astringency. This is the opposite of sweetness and umami, which harden wine perception.

stem: According to the materials, what is the recommended service temperature range for light-bodied red wines such as Beaujolais?

options.text: 15–18°C (59–64°F) — room temperature
17°C (55°F) — lightly chilled

explanation: The service temperature table specifies that **light-bodied reds such as Beaujolais and Valpolicella should be served lightly chilled at around 17°C (55°F)**. Medium/full-bodied reds such as Bordeaux and Barolo are served at room temperature (15–18°C); sparkling and sweet wines are served well chilled at 6–10°C.

stem: What is the recommended long-term storage temperature range for all wines?

options.text: 0–5°C, as cool as possible
10–15°C, cool and constant
18–22°C, slightly above room temperature
Any temperature, as long as it stays constant

explanation: The materials state that for **long-term storage, the temperature for all wines should be cool and constant, preferably between 10°C and 15°C**. Extremes of cold and heat can cause damage. Extended refrigeration can harden corks and cause them to fail; kitchens are specifically cited as one of the worst storage locations due to temperature fluctuations.

stem: Which glass shape is recommended for serving sparkling wines, and why?

options.text: Large-bowled glasses, to allow the bubbles to interact with a large wine surface
Medium-sized glasses, to gather and direct fresh fruit characteristics
Flute glasses, which allow bubbles to travel through a larger volume of wine before bursting at the top
Saucer-shaped glasses, which spread the mousse across a wide surface for maximum aroma release

explanation: The materials recommend **flute glasses for sparkling wines** because the shape allows bubbles to travel through a larger volume of wine before bursting at the top, enhancing the wine's aroma. The old-style saucer-shaped glass is specifically noted as inappropriate because bubbles are very quickly lost in such a shape.

stem: Which general pairing rule applies when a dish contains a significant level of sugar?

options.text: Select a wine with at least as much sweetness as the dish
Select a wine that is drier than the dish to provide contrast
Select a high-acid, low-alcohol wine to cut through the sweetness
Select a full-bodied red wine to balance the richness of the dish

explanation: The materials state clearly that with dishes containing sugar, a good general rule is to **select a wine that has a higher level of sweetness than the dish** (or at least as much). Sweetness in food makes a dry wine seem less fruity and unpleasantly acidic, so a wine that is drier than the dish will suffer by comparison.

stem: What is the correct technique for filling an ice bucket to chill a bottle of wine effectively?

options.text: Fill it with ice only, packing the ice around the bottle tightly
Fill it halfway with ice and add salt to lower the freezing point of the water
Fill it with cold water only, as ice alone is too extreme and can over-chill the wine
Fill it three-quarters full with equal quantities of ice and water so the bottle is surrounded by iced water

explanation: The materials specify that **an ice bucket should be filled three-quarters full with equal quantities of ice and water** so that the bottle is fully surrounded by iced water. Water transfers heat from the bottle to melt the ice; a bottle in ice alone will chill very slowly until some of the ice has melted, because air acts as an insulator.

stem: According to the materials, how many 175 ml glasses can be poured from a standard 75 cl bottle?

options.text: Three
Four
Five
Six

explanation: The materials show that a standard 75 cl bottle yields **4 × 175 ml glasses**. It also yields 6 × 125 ml glasses and 3 × 250 ml glasses. This knowledge helps with calculating how many bottles are needed for a service.

stem: Why does acidity in food generally improve rather than harm food and wine pairing?

options.text: Acidity in food increases the perception of tannin, helping to balance high-tannin red wines
Acidity in food suppresses umami, making the wine taste less bitter and astringent
Acidity in food increases the perception of body, sweetness, and fruitiness while decreasing the perception of acidity in the wine
Acidity in food increases the warming effect of alcohol, helping wines seem more integrated

explanation: The materials explain that **acidity in food increases the perception of body, sweetness, and fruitiness in the wine, while decreasing its perceived acidity**. This means food acidity can bring a very high-acid wine into balance and enhance its fruitiness. The key caveat is that if the wine itself has low acidity, high food acidity can make it seem flat and flabby.

stem: The materials explain why both Muscadet and Champagne pair well with oysters. Which combination of wine characteristics is cited as the reason?

options.text: Both are unoaked, relatively light in flavour, and high in acidity
Both are high in residual sugar, which balances the saltiness of the oyster
Both have prominent toasty oak characteristics that complement the umami of oysters
Both are full-bodied, with high alcohol that cuts through the richness of the shellfish

explanation: The materials cite three reasons for the Muscadet/Champagne and oyster pairing: **both are unoaked** (no bitter tannin to be worsened by the oyster's umami), **relatively light in flavour** (so as not to overwhelm the delicate oyster), and **high in acidity** (so they remain vibrant when oysters are eaten with lemon juice). The materials use this example to illustrate how understanding structural interactions allows other suitable wines to be identified.

stem: Why does chilli heat in food present a particular challenge when pairing with high-alcohol wines?

options.text: High-alcohol wines increase the tannin perception, which is amplified further by chilli heat
Chilli heat reduces a wine's perceived acidity, making high-alcohol wines seem overly sweet
High-alcohol wines suppress the fruitiness that is needed to balance the acidity caused by chilli heat
The effects of chilli heat on bitterness and astringency are greater in wines with high alcohol, and alcohol also increases the burning sensation of chilli

explanation: The materials state that **the effects caused by chilli heat are greater in wines with high alcohol than in those with lower alcohol, and that alcohol also increases the burning sensation of the chilli**. For this reason, chilli dishes should be paired with white wines or low-tannin reds that are neither high in alcohol. Some people enjoy the enhanced burning sensation, but it can easily become unpleasant.

stem: When decanting a wine with a heavy deposit, at what point should pouring stop?

options.text: When the bottle is half empty, to avoid disturbing the deposit in the lower half
When the deposit can be seen near the neck of the bottle
When the wine becomes noticeably darker in colour, indicating deposit has mixed in
When one-quarter of the bottle remains, as a safety margin against deposit

explanation: The materials instruct that when decanting, the bottle should be held in front of a light and wine poured carefully into the decanter until **the deposit can be seen near the neck — at which point pouring must stop**. The bottle is first placed horizontally to allow deposit to settle, and is handled carefully throughout to avoid agitating the sediment.

stem: What does the materials' concept of 'matching or contrasting flavours' actually determine in food and wine pairing, according to their analysis?

options.text: It is the primary driver of whether a pairing succeeds or fails
It is irrelevant because all pairings are ultimately subjective
It can enhance a structurally sound pairing, but cannot save a structurally poor one
It works only for wines with pronounced tertiary aromas, not for young primary-fruit wines

explanation: The materials are explicit: **flavour matching or contrasting can further enhance a pairing if the structural pairing works, but if the structural pairing fails, the match is likely to be unsuccessful**. Success or failure depends primarily on the interaction of structural components — sugar, acid, salt, tannin, alcohol — not on whether the flavours mirror or contrast each other.

stem: Why do wines stored with cork closures need to be stored on their side, while wines with screw caps do not?

options.text: Horizontal storage keeps the cork in contact with the wine, preventing it from drying out and letting air in
Bottles on their side take up less space in a rack, making storage more practical
Horizontal storage reduces the pressure of the wine on the cork, preventing premature oxidation
Screw cap wines develop faster in the bottle when stored upright due to greater oxygen ingress

explanation: The materials state that **wines sealed with cork must be stored on their side to keep the cork in contact with the wine**. If the cork dries out it can let in air, which will oxidise the wine. Screw cap closures do not require this because they create a reliable seal regardless of orientation.

stem: What is the primary reason the 'local wine with local food' principle tends to produce successful pairings?

options.text: Local wines are always lower in tannin and higher in acidity, making them universally food-friendly
Local wines share the same mineral character as local produce, creating flavour harmony
Regional regulations ensure that local wines are specifically blended to match local cuisine
Successful food and wine pairings are likely to have evolved locally over time

explanation: The materials explain that the local wine with local food principle works because **successful pairings are likely to have evolved locally over time** — cuisine and wine styles develop in parallel in the same region. However, the materials also caution that this principle alone is insufficient: most regions produce wines in a range of styles, so structural knowledge of food and wine interactions is still needed to identify the best specific choices.

stem: What practical effect does opening a still wine bottle some time before service ('airing') have on the wine?

options.text: It allows the wine to warm up to room temperature and integrate its aromas
It has no beneficial effect, as too little wine is in contact with the air for it to matter
It softens the tannins and reduces astringency in young red wines
It is beneficial for white wines but should be avoided for red wines

explanation: The materials are clear that **'airing' a wine by opening it some time before service does no good at all**, because too little of the wine is in contact with the air for it to have any effect. For young wines that benefit from aeration, decanting or swirling the wine in the glass is much more effective.

stem: According to the materials, why are foods high in umami considered 'high-risk' for wine pairing, even when the umami level in the food alone seems acceptable?

options.text: Umami is indistinguishable from bitterness on the palate, so it always registers as excessive
Umami suppresses the wine's sweetness and fruitiness, making it impossible to detect these qualities
Bitter elements from both food and wine combine, and together can reach an unpleasant level even when each is acceptable in isolation
High-umami foods coat the palate and prevent the wine from being tasted properly

explanation: The materials explain that **bitter flavours from food and wine add to each other**: bitterness in the food alone may be pleasant, and bitterness in the wine alone may be in balance, but together the combined bitter elements can reach an unpleasant level. This additive effect is why umami-rich foods without salt to counteract them are classified as high-risk. The wine needs sufficient concentrated fruit to cope with the resulting change.

stem: The materials describe a 'low-risk wine' as one unlikely to be made unpleasant by any dish, yet also note a drawback to serving such wines. What is that drawback?

options.text: Low-risk wines change relatively little when paired with food, making the pairing experience less interesting
Low-risk wines are typically expensive, making them impractical for everyday pairing
Low-risk wines tend to overpower delicate dishes because their residual sugar amplifies all flavours
Low-risk wines lose their aromatic character rapidly when served alongside food

explanation: The materials define low-risk wines as simple, unoaked wines with a little residual sugar. While **such wines are unlikely to be made unpleasant by any dish**, the trade-off is that **they change relatively little when partnered with food**, making the food and wine pairing experience less interesting. By contrast, high-risk wines with more structural components offer more possible taste interactions and potentially more rewarding pairings.

stem: The materials challenge the 'red wine with red meat, white wine with fish' rule by identifying when it is most likely to break down. What is the specific case where red wine and fish is most problematic regardless of other factors?

options.text: Any fish dish served with lemon juice, which increases acidity and clashes with red wine tannins
Smoked fish, which has high salt content that reacts negatively with red wine tannins
White fish with umami-rich sauces, which amplify the bitterness of red wine tannins
Oily fish, where compounds in red wine react with the fish to produce a metallic taste

explanation: The materials acknowledge that the red wine with fish rule can sometimes be broken — salt and acid in many fish dishes can counterbalance umami's hardening effect on red wine. However, **compounds in red wine react with oily fish to produce a metallic taste**, and this reaction is described as 'more difficult to avoid'. With oily fish specifically, the materials recommend sticking to white wine.

stem: The materials note that sensitivity to umami can vary greatly between individuals, and that what counts as a perfect pairing for one person may not work for another. What is the practical implication of this for a sommelier or chef?

options.text: Sommeliers should avoid recommending pairings involving umami-rich foods entirely
The best a sommelier can do is find a pairing that most people will find successful, while accepting that guests may disagree
Individual variation means that structural food and wine pairing principles should be abandoned in favour of personal preference
A sommelier should always ask each guest individually whether they are sensitive to umami before making a recommendation

explanation: The materials explicitly state that **there is no such thing as an objectively perfect pairing**, and that the best a sommelier or chef can achieve is something that works for most people while accepting that guests may not all agree. People vary in both their sensitivities and preferences. This is why pairings should be framed as 'most people will find that dish X goes well with wines in style Y' rather than as absolutes.

stem: What is the name of the main Eurasian vine species used in winemaking, responsible for producing nearly all the world's wine grapes?

options.text: Vitis labrusca
Vitis riparia
Vitis vinifera
Vitis rupestris

explanation: **Vitis vinifera** is the main Eurasian species used in winemaking. It produces nearly all the grapes used in winemaking and has been used to make wine for several thousand years. American vine species such as Vitis riparia and Vitis rupestris are important as rootstocks due to their phylloxera resistance, but are rarely used directly for winemaking.

stem: What is the French term for the colour change that signals the start of the ripening process in grapes?

options.text: Véraison
Budburst
Fruit set
Flowering

explanation: **Véraison** is the French term for the colour change in grapes from green to blue/black, pink, or golden (depending on the variety) that marks the first sign of the ripening process. During this stage, grapes start to swell with water, sugar levels rise, acid levels drop, and flavours develop. Budburst marks the start of the growing season; fruit set is when flowers form small grapes.

stem: Why are American vine species important in viticulture if they are rarely used for winemaking?

options.text: They are used for cross-fertilisation to create new disease-resistant V. vinifera varieties
They are used as cover crops between rows to suppress weeds and improve soil health
They produce grapes used for distillation rather than winemaking
They are used as rootstocks onto which V. vinifera vines are grafted, due to their phylloxera resistance

explanation: American vine species are important because, unlike V. vinifera, they are **resistant to phylloxera** — a vine pest that attacks vine roots. They are therefore widely used to produce **rootstocks** onto which V. vinifera vines are grafted. Their wines are widely considered to have unattractive flavours, which is why they are not used directly for winemaking.

stem: Between which latitudes do most of the world's vineyards lie?

options.text: 10° and 20° north and south of the Equator
30° and 50° north and south of the Equator
20° and 40° north and south of the Equator
40° and 60° north and south of the Equator

explanation: The materials state that most vineyards lie **between the latitudes of 30° and 50° north and south of the Equator**. Closer to the Equator it is too hot; closer to the Poles it is too cold. However, factors such as altitude, ocean currents, and aspect can make some sites outside these limits viable for viticulture.

stem: What is a 'clone' in the context of grape growing?

options.text: A new grape variety created by cross-fertilising two different grape varieties
A vine propagated from seed that shares 50% of its DNA with the parent plant
An individual vine or group of vines from a single variety that shows a particular set of unique characteristics due to mutation
A cutting taken from a vine that is trained using a different pruning system than the parent

explanation: In grape growing, **a clone is an individual vine or group of vines within a variety that shows a particular set of unique characteristics** arising from a mutation in the original plant. Clones with positive characteristics — such as better fruit quality or disease resistance — can be selected for propagation. Pinot Blanc and Pinot Gris are cited as examples of mutations significant enough to be treated as separate varieties, though they are strictly clones of Pinot Noir.

stem: What minimum temperature does the vine require before it can begin to grow?
explanation: The materials state that **if the temperature is below 10°C it is too cold for the vine to grow**, which is why vines are dormant in winter and budburst happens in spring when temperatures rise above this threshold. The vine also needs sufficient heat for successful flowering, fruit set, and ripening beyond just initiating growth.

stem: What does 'continentality' refer to in the context of wine growing climates?

options.text: The distance of a vineyard from the nearest ocean or large body of water
The annual rainfall total averaged over several decades
The difference between daytime and night-time temperatures during the growing season
The temperature difference between the coldest and the hottest months of the year

explanation: **Continentality is the temperature difference between the coldest and the hottest months**. Regions with high continentality have large seasonal temperature differences, while those with low continentality see less variation throughout the year. Large bodies of water moderate these extremes, which is why coastal and lakeside regions tend to have low continentality. Diurnal range, by contrast, refers to the difference between daytime and night-time temperatures.

stem: What are the three most important nutrients a vine requires from the soil?

options.text: Calcium, magnesium, and iron
Nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium
Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen
Sulphur, zinc, and manganese

explanation: The materials identify **nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium** as the most important nutrients for a vine. These occur naturally in soil and are absorbed by the vine's roots. Importantly, vines do not need high levels — too many nutrients cause vigorous vegetative growth at the expense of fruit quality. A common symptom of nutrient deficiency is chlorosis, where leaves turn yellow and photosynthesis is restricted.

stem: Why can altitude compensate for proximity to the Equator in viticulture?

options.text: Higher altitudes receive more ultraviolet light, which enhances grape flavour development
Altitude increases rainfall, providing the water supply that grapes need during ripening
Temperatures drop as altitude increases, allowing vines to be grown in regions that would otherwise be too hot
High-altitude vineyards are above the frost line, reducing the risk of spring frost damage

explanation: **As altitude increases, temperatures drop**, which means regions at high altitude can successfully grow vines even when they are close to the Equator where temperatures would otherwise be too high. The materials cite Cafayate in northern Argentina as a specific example — it is close to the Equator but produces quality wines due to its high altitude location.

stem: What is the key viticultural benefit of a large diurnal range (large difference between daytime and night-time temperatures) in a warm or hot climate?

options.text: Cool nights slow the loss of aromas and acidity in the grapes during ripening, producing fresher and more aromatic wines
Warm days and cold nights slow budburst, extending the growing season
Large diurnal range increases tannin ripeness in red varieties, producing softer, rounder wines
Cold nights reduce the vine's water requirements, making irrigation unnecessary even in dry climates

explanation: The materials explain that **cool nights help slow the loss of aromas and acidity in the grapes during ripening**. In a warm or hot region, a vineyard with a large diurnal range can therefore produce wines that are fresher and more aromatic compared with a vineyard that has a smaller diurnal range. Warm nights, by contrast, accelerate ripening and can result in less aromatic, lower-acid wines.

stem: How does excess water during the growing season harm grape quality?

options.text: It causes the vine to redirect sugar from the grapes to the roots, reducing sugar accumulation
It dilutes the natural acids in the grape, producing wines with insufficient freshness
It raises soil pH, limiting the vine's ability to absorb potassium and other nutrients
It promotes continued shoot and leaf growth during ripening and increases canopy shading, restricting grape ripening

explanation: The materials explain that if a vine has access to too much water during the growing season, **it will continue to grow shoots and leaves rather than concentrating on ripening grapes**. The extra shoots and leaves increase shading in the canopy, further restricting grape ripening. In extreme cases, waterlogged soil can cause roots to die. This is why mild water stress after véraison is beneficial — it concentrates the vine's energy on ripening.

stem: What is the key difference between a continental climate and a maritime climate in terms of how they affect viticulture?

options.text: Continental climates are always warmer than maritime climates because they are further from the moderating influence of the ocean
Continental climates have high continentality with large seasonal temperature differences and dry summers; maritime climates have low continentality and more evenly spread rainfall
Continental climates receive more sunlight hours per year than maritime climates
Maritime climates are suitable only for white grape varieties due to their lower temperatures

explanation: The materials define **continental climates** as having high continentality — large differences between the hottest and coldest months — and typically dry summers. **Maritime climates** have low continentality, moderate temperatures, and rainfall spread more evenly throughout the year. Bordeaux is cited as a maritime example where the even rainfall allows Cabernet Sauvignon to ripen even at that latitude, while cool continental climates like Chablis and Champagne are at greater spring frost risk.

stem: What is 'green harvesting' and what risk does it carry if performed at the wrong time?

options.text: Harvesting grapes before full ripeness to retain acidity; the risk is producing wines that are too acidic
Removing leaves around grape clusters to improve sun exposure; the risk is sunburn in hot climates

Removing immature grapes shortly after véraison to reduce yield; the risk is that the vine compensates by increasing the size of remaining grapes, potentially returning yield to its original level and diluting flavours
Harvesting entire vine shoots rather than individual bunches; the risk is damaging the vine's permanent wood

explanation: **Green harvesting** is the practice of removing immature grapes shortly after véraison in order to reduce yields. The risk is that if done at the wrong time, **the vine will compensate for the loss by increasing the size of the remaining grapes**. This can return the yield to its original size and also dilute the flavours in the remaining grapes — the opposite of the intended effect.

stem: Why do soils with high clay content require particular management to avoid problems in viticulture?

options.text: Clay soils bind water tightly and can become waterlogged, potentially killing vine roots in extreme cases
Clay soils reflect too much sunlight and prevent the vine's roots from warming up sufficiently
Clay soils are deficient in nitrogen and phosphorus, leading to chlorosis in young vines
Clay soils promote excessive root growth, making mechanical harvesting impossible

explanation: The materials explain that **water is stored in soil by binding to clay particles**, and that **if a soil contains too much clay it can easily become waterlogged**. In extreme cases, waterlogging can kill vine roots. Planting on slopes or installing drainage pipes are the recommended solutions for waterlogging-prone flat sites. By contrast, sandy soils and stones drain well but may not retain enough water, potentially requiring irrigation.

stem: What are the three main reasons given for using a trellis system in a vineyard?

options.text: To reduce yields, improve water retention in the soil, and protect the vine from hail
To anchor the vine's roots, direct nutrients upwards, and allow closer planting densities
To support the vine's trunk, reduce frost risk, and enable vertical shoot positioning
To control sunlight in the canopy, improve air circulation through the leaves and grapes, and aid mechanisation

explanation: The materials list three key reasons for trellising: **first, to control the amount of sunlight reaching the canopy** (maximising it in cool regions, providing shade in hot ones); **second, to improve air circulation**, which is especially important in wet climates to limit fungal disease; and **third, to aid mechanisation**, by positioning grapes and leaves in separate zones to simplify machine harvesting and targeted spraying.

stem: What is the difference between spur pruning and replacement cane pruning in winter vine pruning?

options.text: Spur pruning retains long canes of up to 20 buds; replacement cane pruning cuts wood back to short stubs of two to three buds
Spur pruning cuts one-year-old wood back to short stubs of two to three buds; replacement cane pruning retains longer sections of one-year-old wood with up to 20 buds
Spur pruning is only used on head-trained vines; replacement cane pruning is only used on cordon-trained vines
Spur pruning is performed in summer; replacement cane pruning is performed in winter

explanation: **Spur pruning** cuts one-year-old wood back to short stubs of just two to three buds, distributed along a cordon or around the trunk. **Replacement cane pruning** retains longer sections of one-year-old wood (canes) with up to 20 buds, tied horizontally to the trellis. Replacement cane pruning is more complex and requires a skilled workforce; it is sometimes referred to as Guyot training.

stem: How does grafting onto American rootstocks protect V. vinifera vines from phylloxera, and what technique is most widely used today?

options.text: American rootstocks produce chemical compounds that repel phylloxera; the bench grafting technique is most widely used
Grafted vines develop thicker root bark that physically prevents phylloxera from penetrating; layering is the most widely used grafting method
American rootstocks are resistant to phylloxera root attack; the most popular modern grafting technique joins a rootstock to a V. vinifera cutting
American rootstocks outcompete phylloxera for soil nutrients; field grafting onto existing vines is most commonly used

explanation: **American vine species are resistant to phylloxera's root attacks**, unlike V. vinifera. Grafting the V. vinifera variety onto an American rootstock therefore protects it from the pest while preserving the winemaking qualities of the V. vinifera variety. The materials describe the most popular modern technique as joining a rootstock to a V. vinifera variety — typically using pre-grafted plants purchased from nurseries. Due to phylloxera risk, most growers now use cuttings rather than layering for propagation.

stem: The materials describe two ocean currents that cool wine regions that might otherwise be too hot. Which currents are cited, and which regions do they affect?

options.text: The Humboldt Current cools Chile; the Benguela Current cools South Africa
The Gulf Stream cools Chile; the Benguela Current cools the north-west of Europe
The Humboldt Current warms Chile; the Gulf Stream cools South Africa
The Benguela Current cools Chile and South Africa; the Gulf Stream cools the Mediterranean

explanation: The materials cite two cooling ocean currents: the **Humboldt Current off Chile** and the **Benguela Current off South Africa**, both of which cool regions that might otherwise be too hot for quality grape growing. By contrast, the Gulf Stream is mentioned as a warming current that benefits north-west Europe, which might otherwise be too cold.

stem: The materials note that a high-fertility soil is not always solved by high-density planting alone in some New World vineyard areas. What alternative approach has been developed for these sites?

options.text: Using drought stress to limit vegetative growth, even on naturally well-watered fertile sites
Harvesting mechanically to reduce the growing season and limit the vine's access to nutrients
Applying chemical growth retardants to restrict shoot growth without reducing planting density
Low-density planting with vines trained to multiple cordons or canes, which allows quality production at high yields

explanation: The materials explain that on highly fertile New World soils, high-density planting is often not enough to limit vine vigour. An alternative solution involves **low-density planting using vines with multiple cordons or canes**. This system is often able to produce good quality grapes at high yields by giving each vine a large root zone while managing the canopy across multiple cordons to distribute vigour and maintain fruit quality.

stem: The materials explain why Mediterranean climates produce wines with characteristics that differ from those of maritime climates. Which combination of climate features drives these differences in wine style?

options.text: High continentality and spring frost risk lead to concentrated, high-acid wines in Mediterranean climates
Warm, dry summers with more warmth and sunlight than maritime climates lead to fuller-bodied wines with riper tannins, higher alcohol, and lower acidity
Low continentality and evenly distributed rainfall produce wines with consistent freshness and structure across vintages
The combination of fog and coastal cooling creates fresher, more aromatic wines than are possible in inland continental climates

explanation: The materials describe Mediterranean climates as having **warm, dry summers and more warmth and sunlight than maritime climates**. This extra warmth and sunlight leads to **wines that are fuller-bodied, with riper tannins, higher alcohol, and lower levels of acidity** compared with maritime regions. Lower rainfall benefits grape health but can also lead to drought. Regions cited include coastal California, Chile, South Africa, and south-eastern Australia.

stem: Why is Merlot the most important grape variety in Saint-Émilion and Pomerol but not in the Haut-Médoc?

options.text: Merlot produces wines with higher tannin than Cabernet Sauvignon and is therefore better suited to the Haut-Médoc's tannic soils
Merlot can successfully grow on the cooler clay soils of Saint-Émilion and Pomerol, where Cabernet Sauvignon struggles to ripen
Merlot ripens earlier than Cabernet Sauvignon and is favoured in the warmer, gravelly soils of the Haut-Médoc
Merlot is permitted only on the Right Bank under Bordeaux appellation regulations

explanation: **Merlot can successfully grow on the cooler clay soils** of Saint-Émilion and Pomerol, whereas Cabernet Sauvignon would struggle to ripen there. The high stone/gravel content of the Haut-Médoc raises vineyard temperatures, making it one of the only areas where Cabernet Sauvignon can reliably ripen. Merlot's softness also makes it the predominant grape in high-volume, inexpensive Bordeaux wines.

stem: Which appellation in Bordeaux is the home of many of the very best dry white wines and is the only area of Graves where whites may be eligible for cru classé status?

options.text: Entre-Deux-Mers
Pauillac
Pessac-Léognan
Barsac

explanation: **Pessac-Léognan** is home to many of the very best dry whites in Bordeaux, where the whites may be eligible for *cru classé* status. They tend to be blends of Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon, fermented and/or matured partly in new oak, giving medium to full body and toasty flavours. Entre-Deux-Mers can only produce white wines but these are typically unoaked and do not achieve *cru classé* status.

stem: What role does Sémillon play in the production of Sauternes?

options.text: It provides high acidity and citrus aromas to balance the sweetness
It adds exotic perfume and floral aromas to the blend
It supplies body and oak character through barrel fermentation
It dominates because of its thin skin and susceptibility to noble rot

explanation: **Sémillon dominates Sauternes because of its thin skin and susceptibility to botrytis** (noble rot). Its thin skin allows the botrytis fungus to penetrate easily, concentrating sugars and producing the distinctive apricot and citrus peel aromas of noble rot. Sauvignon Blanc supplies the refreshing acidity, while Muscadelle adds exotic perfume. Sémillon's role is specifically tied to its physical vulnerability to botrytis.

stem: How does the Saint-Émilion classification system differ from the 1855 Médoc Classification?

options.text: The Saint-Émilion system is integrated into the appellation system and includes reclassifications with the possibility of promotion and demotion at least every ten years
The Saint-Émilion system ranks individual vineyards rather than individual estates, and has five ranks rather than three
The Saint-Émilion system is permanent and has never been revised, unlike the 1855 Classification which was updated in 2012
The Saint-Émilion system ranks estates by market price, as the 1855 Classification originally did

explanation: **The Saint-Émilion classification is integrated into the appellation system** — unlike the 1855 Médoc Classification, which lies outside the appellation system. Crucially, Saint-Émilion has **reclassifications with the possibility of promotion and demotion occurring at least once every ten years**, making it a living classification. The 1855 Classification stands virtually intact and does not have a built-in review mechanism.

stem: What is the key characteristic of Cru Bourgeois as a classification, and how does it differ from Bordeaux's other classifications?

options.text: Cru Bourgeois is awarded to specific vineyard plots rather than châteaux, and is permanent once granted
Cru Bourgeois is awarded to wines in a specific vintage rather than to the château itself, so wines from new vintages must be submitted each year
Cru Bourgeois ranks châteaux in three tiers: Cru Bourgeois, Cru Bourgeois Supérieur, and Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel
Cru Bourgeois is a sub-classification within the 1855 system for châteaux that were originally ranked fifth growth

explanation: **Cru Bourgeois is currently only awarded to wines in a specific vintage, rather than to the château itself**. This means that wines from new vintages must be submitted each year to gain the classification. This vintage-by-vintage approach distinguishes it from the 1855 Classification, where châteaux hold their classification regardless of individual vintage performance.

stem: Why does Petit Verdot play only a minor role in Bordeaux blends, and what specific characteristics does it contribute when used?

options.text: Petit Verdot adds primarily body and softness, and is limited because it is susceptible to noble rot in wet vintages
Petit Verdot is rarely used because it produces wines with herbaceous aromas when unripe, similar to Cabernet Franc
Petit Verdot ripens fully only in very hot years and contributes deep colour, tannin, and some spicy notes
Petit Verdot dominates Left Bank blends in the Haut-Médoc but is limited by appellation rules to a maximum of five per cent

explanation: **Petit Verdot ripens fully only in very hot years**, making it unreliable in Bordeaux's variable climate — hence its minor role. When fully ripe, it produces a very **deep-coloured, tannic wine** that ages slowly and is used in blends mainly to **add tannin, colour, and some spicy notes**. It never plays more than a supporting role. Cabernet Franc, not Petit Verdot, is the variety associated with herbaceous aromas when unripe.

stem: Which sub-region of the Côte d'Or is most famous for producing the greatest white Burgundies, and is home to all the white grands crus except one?

options.text: Côte de Nuits
Côte Chalonnaise
Mâconnais
Côte de Beaune

explanation: The **Côte de Beaune** is most famous for producing the best Chardonnays in the region. Almost all the white *grands crus* come from the Côte de Beaune (with one exception), while almost all the red *grands crus* (except one) come from the Côte de Nuits. The three villages with the highest reputation for white wine are Meursault, Puligny-Montrachet, and Chassagne-Montrachet.

stem: What is the major climatic hazard in Chablis, and what two protective measures are used against it?

options.text: Frost; sprinklers and heaters are both used as protective measures
Hail; the main protections are netting and windbreaks
Drought; irrigation and cover crops are the main protective techniques
Grey rot; canopy management and fungicide spraying are the primary defences

explanation: The major climatic problem in Chablis is **frost**, which can appear as late as May. The two protective measures cited in the materials are **sprinklers** (which release heat as they freeze, protecting buds) and **heaters** (which create air movement to prevent cold air settling). This is a particularly important concern because the region's continental climate makes spring frost a recurring threat.

stem: How can a Burgundy grand cru wine be identified on the label?

options.text: The village name appears prominently and the term 'grand cru' appears beneath it
Only the vineyard name appears on the label, and the term 'grand cru' must appear on the label
The producer's name appears alone on the label with 'grand cru' as a subtitle
Grand cru wines carry both the village name and the vineyard name in equal prominence

explanation: Burgundy *grand cru* wines can be identified because **only the vineyard name appears on the label** — not the village name — and **the term 'grand cru' must also appear**. This distinguishes them from *premier cru* wines, which must state 'premier cru' and may include a vineyard name alongside the village name. There are 33 grands crus in the Côte d'Or and one in Chablis.

stem: Why do Chablis Premier Cru and Grand Cru wines generally show riper, more concentrated fruit than basic Chablis?

options.text: They are made from older vines that produce smaller yields and therefore more concentrated fruit
They are always fermented in new oak barrels, which adds body and richness to the wines
They are located on south-facing hillside sites with a better aspect, giving greater concentration and ripeness
Premier cru and grand cru regulations require lower maximum alcohol levels, concentrating flavour

explanation: Chablis Premier Cru and Grand Cru vineyards are **located on south-facing hillsides with a better aspect**, which gives greater concentration and ripeness compared with basic Chablis and Petit Chablis, which are often grown on north-facing slopes and flatter land. The better exposure produces citrus rather than green apple flavours and more body, though the wines remain balanced by high acidity. Oak is used by some but not all producers at these levels.

stem: What is the appellation rule for Gamay in Burgundy's village-level and above appellations?

options.text: Gamay may be blended with Pinot Noir in village-level wines but not in premier or grand cru wines
Gamay is permitted in all Burgundy appellations as long as it forms no more than 15 per cent of the blend
Gamay is not grown in Burgundy at all — it is restricted to Beaujolais
Only Pinot Noir is allowed for wines of village level and above; Gamay is restricted to regional appellations

explanation: The materials state that **only Pinot Noir is allowed for wines of village level and above** in Burgundy. Gamay can be used in some regional appellations, where it produces early-drinking red wines with ripe berry fruit and low tannins. Gamay is also, of course, the dominant variety in neighbouring Beaujolais, but it is grown in Burgundy and used at the regional level.

stem: What distinguishes Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran from the broader Mâcon and Mâcon Villages appellations in terms of wine style?

options.text: Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran are planted on limestone slopes with east and south-east exposures, and typically display ripe tropical and stone fruit with barrel-derived texture and flavours
Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran are the only Mâconnais appellations permitted to use Pinot Noir as well as Chardonnay
Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran must always be aged in new oak, whereas Mâcon Villages is always unoaked
Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran have grand cru vineyards unlike the rest of the Mâconnais, giving them superior appellation status

explanation: The best wines from **Pouilly-Fuissé and Saint-Véran** are planted on the **limestone slopes of the Roche de Solutré with east and south-east exposures** — amphitheatre-like slopes that act as natural suntraps. This produces some of the richest and ripest Chardonnays in Burgundy, **displaying ripe tropical and stone fruit notes**, with most wines matured for a period in barrel to enhance texture and add toasty flavours. The Mâconnais has no grands crus — these two appellations are simply the most prestigious village-level ACs in the sub-region.

stem: What is the primary grape variety used to produce Beaujolais red wines, and in which soil type do the best examples tend to be grown?

options.text: Pinot Noir, grown on limestone and clay soils
Gamay, grown on granite soils with low nutrient levels
Grenache, grown on schist and slate soils
Gamay, grown on alluvial clay soils in the river valley

explanation: Beaujolais red wine production is dominated by **Gamay**, and the best examples are made from grapes grown in **granite soils with low levels of nutrients**. These low-nutrient soils limit yields, resulting in more concentrated flavours in the grapes. The alluvial plain in the east and south of the region, where basic Beaujolais is produced, has less distinguished soils.

stem: What are the four 'noble' grape varieties of Alsace — the only grapes permitted for Vendanges Tardives, Sélection de Grains Nobles, and grand cru wines?

options.text: Riesling, Chardonnay, Pinot Gris, and Gewurztraminer
Riesling, Pinot Blanc, Sylvaner, and Muscat
Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris, and Muscat
Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris, Auxerrois, and Muscat

explanation: The four noble varieties of Alsace are **Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris, and Muscat**. These are the only grapes that can be used for Vendanges Tardives (VT), Sélection de Grains Nobles (SGN), and grand cru wines. Pinot Blanc, Auxerrois, and Sylvaner are grown in Alsace but do not qualify for these higher classifications.

stem: What is the Muscadet Sèvre et Maine Sur Lie style, and what does the 'sur lie' process contribute to the wine?

options.text: The wine is bottled after extended oak barrel ageing, which gives it toasty, vanilla-flavoured complexity
The wine undergoes malolactic fermentation on its lees, giving a buttery texture and lower acidity
The wine is produced from late-harvested grapes left on the vine past normal ripeness, concentrating sugar
The wine is bottled in the spring following the vintage having spent the winter on its lees, giving a richer texture

explanation: **Muscadet Sèvre et Maine Sur Lie** is bottled in the spring following the vintage, having **spent the winter on its lees** (*lie* is French for lees). This **lees contact gives the wines a richer texture**. The wine is handled very little to retain its delicacy and freshness. Muscadet is normally a light-bodied, high-acid white wine, and the sur lie process is one of its key quality differentiators.

stem: Why is Beaujolais Nouveau released on the third Thursday in November, and which appellations are not permitted to be sold in this way?

options.text: It cannot be released until the third Thursday in November, and wines from the ten Beaujolais crus cannot be sold as Beaujolais Nouveau
It cannot be released until the third Thursday in November, and Beaujolais Villages wines cannot be sold as Beaujolais Nouveau
Release in November is optional; the restriction is that it cannot be sold after 31 August, and all appellations may use the Nouveau designation
It cannot be released until the third Thursday in November, and only Beaujolais appellation wines (not Beaujolais Villages) can use the Nouveau designation

explanation: Beaujolais Nouveau **cannot be released to the consumer until the third Thursday in November** after the vintage. The **ten Beaujolais crus cannot be sold as Beaujolais Nouveau** — the designation is only available for Beaujolais and Beaujolais Villages quality wines. Additionally, it cannot be sold by growers or négociants after the following 31 August, reinforcing its status as a wine for immediate consumption.

stem: What is the key challenge Alsace producers face regarding the labelling of sweetness levels in their wines?

options.text: EU regulations prohibit Alsace producers from using the terms 'dry' or 'sweet' on labels because of inconsistent definitions
There is no official labelling scheme to indicate the level of sweetness, so consumers often have no way to tell whether they are buying a dry or sweet wine
All Alsace wines must be labelled as 'off-dry' because producers are not permitted to make fully dry wines
Grand cru wines must indicate sweetness levels but standard Alsace wines are not required to do so by law

explanation: The materials identify a significant problem in Alsace: **there is no official labelling scheme to indicate the level of sweetness**, meaning consumers often have no indication on the bottle of whether they are buying a dry or sweet wine. This has become more pressing as more wines, particularly at grand cru level, are being made with noticeable sweetness. Even VT wines can range from dry to medium-sweet without any indication of this on the label. Knowing the producer's house style is therefore very important.

stem: In Chenin Blanc wines of the Loire, what is the relationship between grape ripeness at harvest and the style of wine produced?

options.text: Less ripe grapes produce sweeter wines because fermentation stops before all the sugar is consumed
Ripeness level determines colour intensity, with the sweetest wines showing the deepest golden colour
The variety of styles — sparkling, dry, medium, and sweet — directly reflects increasingly ripe berries, from barely ripe fruit used for sparkling to botrytis-affected fruit used for sweet wines
The style of wine is determined by the winemaker's choice of yeasts rather than by grape ripeness at harvest

explanation: The materials explain that the variety of Chenin Blanc styles is **directly related to the degree of ripeness at picking**. Barely ripe fruit (producing only 10–11% abv) is used for **sparkling wines**, while **dry, medium, and sweet still wines** reflect the use of increasingly ripe berries — including those shrivelled in the sun or affected by noble rot for the sweetest styles. This single variety therefore produces a remarkable range of styles entirely driven by harvest decisions.

stem: Moulin-à-Vent and Morgon are considered the most structured of the Beaujolais crus. What two factors explain why they are more age-worthy than Brouilly and Fleurie?

options.text: They are always aged in new oak barrels, and their vineyards have warmer south-facing aspects than the other crus
They use carbonic maceration exclusively, which preserves more tannin than crushed-fruit fermentation
They are produced from older vines only, and the appellation rules require a minimum of two years' ageing before release
Their fruit concentration and level of tannins are higher, meaning these wines can improve with bottle ageing

explanation: The materials identify **fruit concentration and higher tannin levels** as the two factors that allow Moulin-à-Vent and Morgon to improve with bottle ageing. By contrast, Brouilly and Fleurie tend to produce lighter, more perfumed styles. Many Beaujolais cru wines undergo crushed-fruit fermentation (not carbonic maceration exclusively), and while some receive oak ageing, this is usually in large vats rather than new oak casks.

stem: What geographic feature shelters Alsace's vineyards from the prevailing westerly rain-bearing winds, resulting in sunny summers and dry autumns?

options.text: The Vosges Mountains
The Rhine river valley
The Massif Central
The Pyrenees

explanation: The **Vosges Mountains** shelter Alsace's vineyards from the prevailing westerly, rain-bearing winds. The resulting lack of clouds produces sunny summers and dry autumns, ensuring grapes achieve high levels of sugar ripeness. In the driest years, drought can even be an issue. This is also why organic and biodynamic viticulture is widely practised in Alsace — the dry conditions mean low disease pressure.

stem: What distinguishes Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé from the majority of Sauvignon Blanc produced in Touraine?

options.text: Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé are the only Loire appellations permitted to ferment Sauvignon Blanc in new oak barrels
Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé are in the Central Vineyards on chalky, well-drained stony soils, producing higher-prestige, more concentrated wines than Touraine Sauvignon Blanc
Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé permit blending Sauvignon Blanc with Chenin Blanc, giving them a more complex style
Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé are located in the Anjou-Saumur sub-region and have warmer, drier conditions than Touraine

explanation: Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé are located in the **Central Vineyards** sub-region on **chalky, well-drained stony soils**, and are the two most prestigious Sauvignon Blanc appellations in the Loire. The majority of Sauvignon Blanc production is from Touraine, where wines tend to have less concentration and are usually made in a simple fruity style. Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé wines are dry with high acidity and can hint at green apple, wet stones, and (particularly for Pouilly-Fumé) subtle smoky notes.

stem: The Pays d'Oc IGP is described as vital for the commercial survival of grape growing in southern France. What two regulatory advantages over appellation wines does it offer producers?

options.text: Lower minimum alcohol levels and permission to irrigate without restriction
Exemption from EU quality controls and permission to blend wines from multiple countries
Permission to use a wider range of grape varieties and to use varietal labelling
Guaranteed minimum prices for growers and permission to add oak chips to wines

explanation: The materials identify two key regulatory advantages of the Pays d'Oc IGP over appellation wines: **a wider range of grape varieties is permitted**, and **varietal labelling is allowed**.
